name: student motivation and engagement
top_issues: C10 (Wayground Value Prop = 2): Wayground is referenced three times but never with a specific, named feature. 'Wayground's accommodations make this automatic' and 'Wayground adapts to your teaching style with standards-aligned resources, built-in accommodations, and time-saving differentiation tools' are generic claims — a teacher reading this cannot picture what they would actually click on or do inside the product. Replace at least one mention with a named feature and a concrete use case (e.g., 'Wayground's Create with AI lets you generate a three-level choice board in under 3 minutes').

C9 (Standards Alignment = 2): This is a lesson-design article that repeatedly mentions 'standards-aligned resources' and 'aligned to standards,' yet zero specific frameworks are named — no Common Core, NGSS, ISTE, or state standards. For a K-12 instructional strategy article, that omission is conspicuous. Add at least one named framework with a clear connection (e.g., 'Common Core's emphasis on text complexity directly supports tiered reading materials').

C5 (Grade-Band Specificity = 3): The article leans heavily on middle school framing ('every middle schooler needs') and the Ms. Patel 7th-grade science anecdote, but the title and meta promise 'every learner' and 'K-12.' Elementary and high school teachers will feel the article wasn't written for them. Add at least one concrete example each for a lower elementary grade band (e.g., K-2) and a high school course to validate the K-12 claim.
positive_signals: The strategy table (mastery-based progressions, choice boards, retakes, progress dashboards, teacher-approved alternatives) is genuinely classroom-ready — a teacher could screenshot it for a planning meeting Monday morning.

Teacher agency is handled well throughout: phrases like 'you know your classroom best' and 'the right tools should amplify your expertise, not complicate it' position the teacher as decision-maker, and the Ms. Patel anecdote grounds the theory in a real classroom outcome.
narrative: This article has strong bones — the strategy table is photocopy-worthy, the structure is easy to scan during a planning period, and the teacher-voice framing is mostly solid — but two criteria score at 2 and trigger a mandatory REVISE. Wayground is name-dropped three times without ever identifying a specific feature a teacher could actually use, and a lesson-design article that talks about "standards-aligned resources" while naming zero standards frameworks will lose credibility with any teacher who reads carefully. Fix those two issues first, then broaden the grade-band examples beyond 7th-grade science to back up the K-12 promise in the title and meta description. Once those gaps are closed, this article is close to a classroom-ready PASS.
